story: Sure South Atlantic Ltd ("Sure") offers television for the island via 17 analogue terrestrial UHF channels, offering a mix of British, US, and South African programming. The channels are from DSTV and include Mnet, SuperSport and BBC channels. The feed signal, from MultiChoice DStv in South Africa, is received by a satellite dish at Bryant's Beacon from Intelsat 7 in the Ku band.
q1: Sure South Atlantic offers satellite television programming from the United States. (true/false)
q2: The television feed is received from a receiver at Bryant's Beacon. (true/false)

story: A satellite ground station with a 7.6-metre (25 ft) satellite dish installed in 1989 at The Briars is the only international connection providing satellite links through Intelsat 707 to Ascension island and the United Kingdom. Since all international telephone and internet communications are relying on this single satellite link both internet and telephone service are subject to sun outages.
q1: The ground at The Briars provides the only international link for the island. (true/false)
q2: Multiple backup points provide international connections. (true/false)

story: Saint Helena has the international calling code +290 which, since 2006, Tristan da Cunha shares. Saint Helena telephone numbers changed from 4 to 5 digits on 1 October 2013 by being prefixed with the digit "2", i.e. 2xxxx, with the range 5xxxx being reserved for mobile numbering, and 8xxx being used for Tristan da Cunha numbers (these still shown as 4 digits).
q1: Saint Helena phone numbers expanded from 4 to 5 characters in 2013. (true/false)
q2: Saint Helena has an exclusive code not shared with others. (true/false)

story: Sports played on the island include football, cricket, volleyball, tennis, golf, motocross, shooting sports and yachting. Saint Helena has sent teams to a number of Commonwealth Games. Saint Helena is a member of the International Island Games Association. The Saint Helena cricket team made its debut in international cricket in Division Three of the African region of the World Cricket League in 2011.
q1: The Saint Helena team made its sporting debut in the World League in 2011. (true/false)
q2: Saint Helena participates in all major global athletic competitions. (true/false)

story: The Governor's Cup is a yacht race between Cape Town and Saint Helena island, held every two years in December/January; the most recent event was in December 2010. In Jamestown a timed run takes place up Jacob's Ladder every year, with people coming from all over the world to take part.
q1: The Governor's Cup is a biennial sailing event between Cape Town and the island. (true/false)
q2: The most recent Governor's Cup competition occurred in 2012. (true/false)

story: There are scouting and guiding groups on Saint Helena and Ascension Island. Scouting was established on Saint Helena island in 1912. Lord and Lady Baden-Powell visited the Scouts on Saint Helena on the return from their 1937 tour of Africa. The visit is described in Lord Baden-Powell's book entitled African Adventures.
q1: Lord and Lady Baden-Powell visited the Scouts on Saint Helena in 1937. (true/false)
q2: Scouting was introduced to Saint Helena by Lord Baden-Powell. (true/false)

story: In phonetics, aspiration is the strong burst of breath that accompanies either the release or, in the case of preaspiration, the closure of some obstruents. In English, aspirated consonants are allophones in complementary distribution with their unaspirated counterparts, but in some other languages, notably most Indian and East Asian languages, the difference is contrastive.
q1: Aspirated consonants in English are allophones in complementary distribution. (true/false)
q2: In English, aspirated and unaspirated sounds differ contrastively. (true/false)

story: Voiceless consonants are produced with the vocal folds open (spread) and not vibrating, and voiced consonants are produced when the vocal folds are fractionally closed and vibrating (modal voice). Voiceless aspiration occurs when the vocal cords remain open after a consonant is released. An easy way to measure this is by noting the consonant's voice-onset time, as the voicing of a following vowel cannot begin until the vocal cords close.
q1: Voiceless consonants are produced with open, non-active vocal folds. (true/false)
q2: Voice-onset time cannot measure aspiration effectively. (true/false)

story: Armenian and Cantonese have aspiration that lasts about as long as English aspirated stops, in addition to unaspirated stops. Korean has lightly aspirated stops that fall between the Armenian and Cantonese unaspirated and aspirated stops as well as strongly aspirated stops whose aspiration lasts longer than that of Armenian or Cantonese. (See voice-onset time.)
q1: Korean has strongly aspirated sounds lasting longer than Armenian or Cantonese. (true/false)
q2: Armenian aspiration extends much longer than English aspirated consonants. (true/false)

story: When aspirated consonants are doubled or geminated, the stop is held longer and then has an aspirated release. An aspirated affricate consists of a stop, fricative, and aspirated release. A doubled aspirated affricate has a longer hold in the stop portion and then has a release consisting of the fricative and aspiration.
q1: An aspirated affricate has a stop, element, and aspirated ending. (true/false)
q2: Doubled aspirated consonants lack any aspirated ending component. (true/false)

story: Industrial production is mainly from the steam reforming of natural gas, and less often from more energy-intensive hydrogen production methods like the electrolysis of water. Most hydrogen is employed near its production site, with the two largest uses being fossil fuel processing (e.g., hydrocracking) and ammonia production, mostly for the fertilizer market. Hydrogen is a concern in metallurgy as it can embrittle many metals, complicating the design of pipelines and storage tanks.
q1: Most hydrogen is used far from its generation location. (true/false)
q2: Water electrolysis is the main industrial hydrogen method. (true/false)

story: Oxidation of hydrogen removes its electron and gives H+, which contains no electrons and a nucleus which is usually composed of one proton. That is why H+ is often called a proton. This species is central to discussion of acids. Under the Bronsted-Lowry theory, acids are proton donors, while bases are proton acceptors.
q1: Oxidation of hydrogen yields H+, which is electron-free. (true/false)
q2: Under Bronsted-Lowry theory, bases donate particles. (true/false)

story: To avoid the implication of the naked "solvated proton" in solution, acidic aqueous solutions are sometimes considered to contain a less unlikely fictitious species, termed the "hydronium ion" (H₃O⁺). However, even in this case, such solvated hydrogen cations are more realistically conceived as being organized into clusters that form species closer to H₉O₄⁺. Other oxonium ions are found when water is in acidic solution with other solvents.
q1: Solvated hydrogen particles exist as aggregates in solutions. (true/false)
q2: The term ionium never describes acidic solutions. (true/false)

story: In the same year the first hydrogen-cooled turbogenerator went into service with gaseous hydrogen as a coolant in the rotor and the stator in 1937 at Dayton, Ohio, by the Dayton Power & Light Co.; because of the thermal conductivity of hydrogen gas, this is the most common type in its field today.
q1: The hydrogen-cooled turbogenerator began operation in the 1920s. (true/false)
q2: The first hydrogen-cooled device was located in Ohio. (true/false)

story: The nickel hydrogen battery was used for the first time in 1977 aboard the U.S. Navy's Navigation technology satellite-2 (NTS-2). For example, the ISS, Mars Odyssey and the Mars Global Surveyor are equipped with nickel-hydrogen batteries. In the dark part of its orbit, the Hubble Space Telescope is also powered by nickel-hydrogen batteries, which were finally replaced in May 2009, more than 19 years after launch, and 13 years over their design life.
q1: Nickel-hydrogen batteries were first tested on a military spacecraft. (true/false)
q2: The Hubble Space Telescope never used nickel-hydrogen power sources. (true/false)

story: An alloy of aluminium and gallium in pellet form added to water can be used to generate hydrogen. The process also produces alumina, but the expensive gallium, which prevents the formation of an oxide skin on the pellets, can be re-used. This has important potential implications for a hydrogen economy, as hydrogen can be produced on-site and does not need to be transported.
q1: Hydrogen generation using aluminium and gallium occurs on-site. (true/false)
q2: Gallium is discarded after the hydrogen process. (true/false)

story: Hydrogen is highly soluble in many rare earth and transition metals and is soluble in both nanocrystalline and amorphous metals. Hydrogen solubility in metals is influenced by local distortions or impurities in the crystal lattice. These properties may be useful when hydrogen is purified by passage through hot palladium disks, but the gas's high solubility is a metallurgical problem, contributing to the embrittlement of many metals, complicating the design of pipelines and storage tanks.
q1: Hydrogen dissolves easily in certain metals. (true/false)
q2: High hydrogen absorption benefits engineering designs. (true/false)

story: In more recent applications, hydrogen is used pure or mixed with nitrogen (sometimes called forming gas) as a tracer gas for minute leak detection. Applications can be found in the automotive, chemical, power generation, aerospace, and telecommunications industries. Hydrogen is an authorized food additive (E 949) that allows food package leak testing among other anti-oxidizing properties.
q1: Nitrogen and hydrogen combined serve for leak detection. (true/false)
q2: Hydrogen is used only in agriculture. (true/false)

story: Hydrogen's rarer isotopes also each have specific applications. Deuterium (hydrogen-2) is used in nuclear fission applications as a moderator to slow neutrons, and in nuclear fusion reactions. Deuterium compounds have applications in chemistry and biology in studies of reaction isotope effects. Tritium (hydrogen-3), produced in nuclear reactors, is used in the production of hydrogen bombs, as an isotopic label in the biosciences, and as a radiation source in luminous paints.
q1: Tritium is artificially produced in atomic facilities. (true/false)
q2: Deuterium lacks practical scientific applications. (true/false)

story: Even interpreting the hydrogen data (including safety data) is confounded by a number of phenomena. Many physical and chemical properties of hydrogen depend on the parahydrogen/orthohydrogen ratio (it often takes days or weeks at a given temperature to reach the equilibrium ratio, for which the data is usually given). Hydrogen detonation parameters, such as critical detonation pressure and temperature, strongly depend on the container geometry.
q1: Hydrogen properties do not depend on the parahydrogen ratio. (true/false)
q2: Container design influences hydrogen detonation behavior. (true/false)
